However, we should probably instead check if the values are numbers and if not then try to convert them to float since the user may use non-strings or non-numbers that could support conversion to float.

This will tie into whatever policy we decide to implement for handling of non-convertible types. Generally, I think it better if we raise an exception if a non-convertible object is passed in. With that in mind, I think we should do the following:

- If value is `None`, convert to a default value.
- If value is `not pyd.is_number(val)`, then try to convert it to a float but `pass` on any exception.
- If float conversion fails, go ahead with operation and let it throw it's exception (i.e. we don't need to throw it explicitly). This way the exception occurs during the operation and not the attempted float conversion.

@scottpurdy `TESTEQUAL2_STR` is defined as `EXPECT_STREQ` in [Tester.hpp](https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/master/src/nupic/test/Tester.hpp#L158). However, in some cases I've changed EXPECTs to ASSERTs. I don't think it's critical which one is used. As I understand it, both types lead to test failure, but ASSERT stops test execution immediately, whereas EXPECT continues to execute the test and it only fails at the end. So you can have multiple EXPECT failures but only one ASSERT failure in a given test. From what I've read, it's considered best practice to use EXPECT except in cases where moving past that point would lead to an unrecoverable error like a segfault.

This is a classic markdown [bug](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/15078393/begin-ordered-list-from-0-in-markdown)-that-they-think-is-a-[feature](http://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/syntax#list).  They finally fixed this on [StackOverflow](http://meta.stackexchange.com/questions/137077/cant-start-a-numbered-list-on-a-number-other-than-1), but [GitHub still forces you](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/user/markdown.html#lists) to start lists at 1.

The workarounds that I know about are
0. put a `\` before the dot: `0\.`, etc. 
0. use a right paren instead: `0)`.  Or `(0)` perhaps.
0. renumber everything starting with 1.
0. use [custom html code](http://stackoverflow.com/a/15078486/1332281)

The prior acceptance tests are a bit of a mixed bag on this because I refined my approach along the way. But as I came to see the "end-user" for this library as the developer using the API, I started using language in the acceptance tests that was more directly sensible to a developer rather than a typical "application" end-user.

So these have become: 'When I assign <value> to tab_stop.position' (or whatever the property is)

This makes them easy to write because the only thing that changes is the object.property part. It also makes them easier to make sense of in the steps .py file.

* The "Feature:" description needs to be more descriptive. There's a pattern to it, you can look at the other feature files to see a bunch of examples.
* There's vertical spacing between the Feature description and the Scenarios (2 blank lines). Check out the others for examples. Basically each section in the .feature files is separated by 2 blank lines.
* Each scenario (Given: block) has its own title. It should also be descriptive. Check out some of the other feature files.
* Scenarios are separated by two blank lines
* I've renamed this file `sct-access-header.feature` This will eventually hold the .first_page_header, .footer, etc. scenarios when we get to them.
* We should put the other two in a separate file named `hdr-props.feature`. A general rule of thumb is scenarios that start with 'Given an x ...' should go in a file that starts with the three-letter abbreviation for x. But lets leave them out for now and stick to this first one.
* Acceptance tests that don't pass yet need the `@wip` decorator. This allows them to be skipped during the full test run while their implementation is work in progress. A flag on the command line tells behave whether to skip them or not, something like '--tags=-wip'. You run them when you're looking for the next error and skip them when regression testing before merge.

`filters` performs exact matching. The highlighted code above has the same effect as this:

```python
orgs = entities.Organization().search()
orgs = [org for org in orgs if org.name == 'foo']
```

This:

```python
Organization(name='foo').search()
```

...passes a search term to the server and lets the server conduct the search. The latter is vastly more efficient.

Unfortunately, `search_payload` is very naive right now. Here's what it'll do:

```python
>>> from nailgun import entities
>>> entities.Organization(name='foo').search_payload()
{'name': 'foo'}
```

And unfortunately, that search will fail. It'll fail silently, in fact - Satellite will respond to this search by returning **every single organization**.
This will fail if the user have Python 2.x installed.

@srowen I think it's a little weird if the user uses Spark master control the core number while the program will not actually use Spark...

One of the differences between spark local and the local mode is that spark local will still use spark component(e.g. RDD, BlockManager) on a single node, while local mode will directly work on arrays. See [LocalOptimizer](https://github.com/intel-analytics/BigDL/blob/master/spark/dl/src/main/scala/com/intel/analytics/bigdl/optim/LocalOptimizer.scala). This will bring some performance difference when training some **small** models. For example, when train LeNet, the local mode can get a 4000+ throughput on my desktop, but spark local can only get 2000+. When you train some bigger model(spend more time on model forward/backward), the perf reduce become less as the overhead of spark become relatively smaller.

We still recommend the user to use spark local when development on the single node. But the local mode is still kept and used in some of our internal tests.

Ideally, I think we can use a JVM property to replace the DL_CORE_NUMBER. But keep it temperately is also fine to me.
